@pr0cessa  I think GAGA was her true succesor. Her music from 2008 to 2015 was very much a throwback to Madonna circa late 80s and 90s. Taboo breaking and empowering. 

Whilst GAGA has gone on to focus half her time in film and tv (an area Madonna had less success in), I think she's the true heir to female pop. Lil Nas X, Miley Cyrus, Dua Lipa, Harry Styles and Billie Eilish etc all have a piece of her in them but Gaga is the heir. 

It's no conincidence that just as Gaga reached icon level famous, Madonna began to struggle to sell singles. She was usurped from the throne. 

For those today in their teens and early 20s Madonna is "dated" but she remains the blueprint to many starts today. 

Those like me late 20s to early 30s were the last to experience the Madonna era yet young enough to enjoy the Gaga era.

Regardless Vogue, Like a prayer, hung up, papa don't preach and ray of light will remain playing at clubs and parties for decades to come even if younger people don't appreciate the woman behind the songs fully. 

This performance even today at a major award show with some modern lighting would be just as jaw dropping

content: Madonna is quite a espectacle!

80’s and 90’s were the peak of humanity. It was a time that technology moved steady, not too fast to invade us even though at times we felt like it was, but nothing like today. There was hope, we seemed hopeful for the future we didn’t fear new things. It was a time that Information was able to spread fast, but not too fast, we had moderation. We will never be at a point like that again. Life still felt like it had mystery, like there were things to learn and we had to seek out information. We weren’t constantly bombarded with consumerism in the same way we are today. People had blissful ignorance, which sorry to admit was such a good feeling at times, we felt free. The best example that I can give is with music, as great as streaming is, the discovery aspect is nowhere near as profound as it used to be. The hunt to try to find music and discovery at a music store, and the absolute jubilation felt when you found something you loved. We still feel this today, but I constantly feel like i’m chasing that same feeling again…things just feel dull sometimes now.

content:
What I like about Madonna is that her voice isn't conventionally perfect. To me, that makes her one of the few female pop stars that sounds human. It's much more pleasing than listening to someone get their Christina Aguilera on (no disrespect to her, though, she's cool too). 

Madonna has a very emotionally resonating voice that sends chills down my spine way more than someone trying to show off their vocal acrobatics. It's very sultry, sweet, warm, and strangely motherly, yet tough and aggressive at times. On acoustic ballads such as "Secret" and many of the deep cuts from "American Life", she kind of reminds me of Karen Carpenter. 

While Madonna may not have the best range, I don't think it's fair to say that she has no vocal talent as a singer, and it's amazing to see how her vocals have evolved and matured over the years.

This is my all time favorite track. It is so timeless, spiritual, liberating and empowerig. There is so much truth in it. You can't carry life's baggage with you to everywhere you go, but you can keep and carry love in your heart and mind regardless of where you are. Love travels and stays with you. It also motivates and energizes you and makes you overcome what life throws at you. The video is far ahead of its time. It is about making it through chaos and spiritual rebirth through love. As the fourth single from Ray of light album, its extended dance CD topped the sales chart as well as the dance club chart. It made it into hot 100, but didn't go all the way to top 40 because the radios were still playing the other tracks from the Ray of Light album. This song didn't get enough radio airplay that it deserved. It is one of Madonna's greatest tracks ever. I love her voice, especially on this track. Lets give this extraordinary gem of a track finally enough love and appreciation that it deserves by revisiting it again and again and by playing it everywhere.

Yes! I was 10 in 1999. I snuck into "The Talented Mr.Ripley"  after buying tickets for "My Dog Skip". Seeing "Fight Club", "The Matrix" posters everywhere. Everyone wondering about Y2K. It was a great time to be a kid! Albums were off the charts too!

A humble suggestion to Madonna:
Album name: ‘Zephyr in the Sky’
An appeal by the fans to release a compilation of 20 masterpieces from Madonna’s catalogue personifying every emotion we have been through in this most dark age. And as we steer back to humanity and life. Search for the future.
A journey to the light, there is always a ray of hope and then the brilliant radiance of life.
Emotions and these songs clutch in perpetuity, the journey we continue.
 
 
Ray of Light
The deliverance of hope. The rhythm of life never seizes. There will always be that zephyr in the sky each night. Embrace it we can.
 
Masterpiece
Preservation of our most precious in peril. Our children, our people. Our kind. Caress them, protect them, enliven them.
 
Crazy
Souls without direction. Souls succumbing to the anarchy. Moments of lapse and dissolution of reason. There is always a method in any madness.
 
Miles Away
Separation - a reality, distancing in the new remote ecosystem – a way of life. Now from a far. Life anew.
 
4 Minutes
There were heroes. And there were those who saved the world, those who ultimately will and those who arrogantly don’t. We can all be heroes, for even more than a day.
 
Borderline
Barriers, walls, borders can neither prevent the triumphant human, nor conquer the invisible enemy. Humanity on the borderline of distraught. Never destroyed.
 
Die Another Day
Death can never be normalized. Not one, not a million. Fight. Death will come, in victory there will ultimately be death. But another day, its not our time to go.
 
You’ll See
Strength is personal, strength is contagion, strength prevails, strength is now our constant. You’ll see.
 
La Isla Bonita
My dreams are always my own and even I don’t control them. There will be sun again, there will be celebration again, we will hold hands again –into that beautiful sunset.
 
Ghost Town
The cradles of our civilization – devoid of voices, people, laughter, love. Ghostly in silence, our great cities shall live again – thrive they shall.
 
Like a Prayer
God. Life is a mystery, everyone must stand alone. You will hear the voice and I always hear you call my name. Forgive me. Pray.
 
Joan of Arc
You’re only human. Our weakness it can never be. A Joan in us all. New awakenings.
 
Sorry

🕰️ Why do people born between 1975 and 1985 feel so nostalgic?

Because they were the last generation to grow up fully offline — playing outside, using cassette tapes, watching TV with just a few channels, writing letters by hand, and hanging out face-to-face.

Then suddenly… boom 💥 — adulthood hit, and the world went digital: social media, smartphones, AI, remote everything. It was a massive shift.

Back then, everyone shared the same culture — same shows, same songs, same toys — which created a deep sense of connection. Today, everything is ultra-personalized and fragmented.

Add to that the fact that a lot of people in this generation are now in their 40s (a time when happiness tends to dip a little 😅), and nostalgia becomes a safe emotional space to return to.

Oh — and it’s not just in your head. The media and entertainment industries know nostalgia sells. That’s why there are so many reboots, retro playlists, and 80s/90s throwbacks everywhere. It’s designed to hit you right in the feels.

👉 In the end, this longing for the past is a way to hold on to what felt real and human, in a world that’s moving faster and getting more digital every day.

Imagine achieving a top 10 hit in Billboard Hot 100, a #1 album in Billboard 200, the biggest tour of the year and the most watched Super Bowl Halftime Show ever in the 30th year of career and being 54 yrs old. Only Madonna.

Yeah 80s rocked.

I think it had a slightly friendlier, gentler vibe and people weren't afraid to just have all sorts of ridiculous fun. Girl's tried big hair all sorts of styles, not like all the same old flat hair and dingy hipster 'style' of today. Guys were not so paranoid about losing street cred and wouldn't get mocked for having fun to listening to stuff like this.

Sometime in the 90s it kinda changed. Between downer, depressing grunge; rough edged gangster rap; news media going nuts with scare stories to bring in ratings and then 9/11 adding to that and so on and so forth the vibe changed a little. Loved the 80s vibe and time. Such a fun, totally optimistic time. If it was fun, people did it and didn't care about what you'd mocked for now as acting too corny or whatnot. In a certain way people today seem more conservative and more like parents of 80s generation kids did (although certainly in a few ways even less conservative).

Back then you could associate with mainstream 'in' pop culture and crowd  words like fun, upbeat, trusting, silly, colorful, bright was like the in thing in pop culture. Then grunge came in and became the mainstream 
in crowd and just enough in that crowd mocked all that fun 80s stuff as being shallow, empty, corny, ridiculous that the tone changed and they brought in dark drab ugly non-styles and a vibe more focused on angst, darkness, anger, depression (which the  hipsters sort of have carried on in a way although maybe without the depressive bent but, at least with some although certainly not all, the mocking and sneering at stuff and, with basically all, the drab zero style and, with a few, the ready to be up in arms over everything and demanding safe spaces and so on) and hardcore hip-hop also became huge and gave a bit rougher edge to the feel of mainstream crowds.

It's honestly hard to put into words, but there was just something a little different. I was on campus both very end of the 80s and very end of the 90s/just into 00s and there was just something more fun and nicer about the earlier period. People just seemed less uptight somehow, more open, chill, upbeat, willing to be fun in (what would now be considered) ridiculous way. And I guess just the styles seemed happier and more fun and unique earlier. Later everything was like drab brown and greasy, flat hair and all dingy and there were more people who were more in your face aggressive and everyone is always ready to make a big stink and jump all over someone or instantly read the worst into everything. I mean many are not like that now, but enough are to make the overall feel different. In the second period too I could swear the 'it' girls seemed mostly into just one certain type of bad-ass gangster poser while in the 80s they seemed to be into a much wider variety of types. Anyway, I mean sure people were still cool and there was fun of course, but I don't man, it just wasn't the 80s.

I've seen the 70s, 80s, 90s, 00s, 10s and I'd take 80s as the best, most fun, relaxed, chill time of them all by far, especially around mid-'83 into around 1993 if had to pick a ten year period.

The 70s were a little drab (like after grunge came in the 90s), a good number of the hippies had turned into more burned out mindless zombies than happy flower children by the end of their run, you had the Vietnam disaster still, so many people smoked it was disgusting (even kids who went to like Harvard and such smoked back then) and they were like old times, no modern slang, no modern tech, no modern sort of pop culture (the 80s really brought us all that, into modern tech and pop culture times). I mean great times if you were maybe a little kid, simple times for that, but no way I'd rather be in HS, college, 20-something in the 70s than 80s and probably not even than today for that matter.

The 80s had modern tech, home computers, CDs, video games, dial-up bulletin boards, portable music, etc. but it wasn't overdone with people glued to cellphone screens 24-7 like ever since the smart phone came out. People actually noticed each other walking around hah. And these days it seems like everything is too over-polished and like everything has been through 30 focus groups instead of maybe 1. So it's like the same pop culture of the 80s but now a little over, over polished, slick, focus grouped, auto-tuned, controlled and with all the really colorful, wild fun edges muted and made drabber and duller and with a dose of fear and paranoia and aggression and uptightness added to the mix.

Anyway I know, tl;dr haha. Another difference is that in the 80s, on the old BBS systems and even on the early internet people would routinely write messages this long, now it's nearing on coming across as a mad man to type more than 144 characters, much less more than a single, full paragraph.

***** As a matter of fact a great deal of Madonna's success comes from her trendsetting attitude, be it 30 years ago or now. Madonna has always been like this...she never follows rules and norm because she creates her own rules and sets her own trends. A major part of her legendary legacy consists of Madonna being the ever trendsetter that she is in both entertainment and fashion worlds. It doesn't matter if she is as influential and successful as she was before but the fact is that she kept true to herself in every sense of it. Of course adaptation and reinvention go hand in hand with it, just like a new gadget that keeps on reinventing. And as of right now, she is creating a new rule on age. She's fighting against the ageist battles for the benefit of all female artists, and paving the way so that younger artists won't have to do it in the future. Reinventing and trendsetting have always been part of her legacy. Her collaboration with other singers, producers, DJ, etc. in her latest album, Rebel Heart also shows that Madonna has established a new trend for herself. People in the music industry (people who matter) know about it. Even her grills is a fashion statement. And like I said my opinion is based on common knowledge.
General public may say that she's not selling anymore and bla bla bla but her latest album Rebel Heart has debuted at number 1 in more than 10 countries worldwide and is among the top 15 best-selling albums of the year. People have been saying that she's over for the last 10 (or maybe more) years and yet she keeps on adding dates to her latest tour, that will surely end up in the top 10 tours of all time, just like her Sticky & Sweet tour and her MDNA tour. Also, tickets for her Rebel Heart tour has sold out in a matter of minutes like hot cakes in many countries. The fact that younger artists (like Taylor Swift, Nicki Minaj, Miley Cyrus, etc.) adoring her and performing live with her; as well as famous celebrities appearing in her music video goes to show that Madonna is still influential and is far from over. Even though Rebel Heart debuted at number 2 on Billboard and although her singles from Rebel Heart did not chart as high on Billboard as they did on iTunes, it doesn't mean that she is over. She's been in the business for over 30 years and for someone like her to still do relatively well in it is simply amazing. Her latest album might not necessarily appeal to new generation music listeners but one has to consider the fact that Madonna cannot cater to everyone's needs. And I know how bad it can be when it comes to the taste of music that my generation has. Madonna is an artist who makes music because it's her passion and it's personal to her. She makes music for others to relate to and she makes music to express herself to the world in a creative and meaningful fashion. Looking at the new generation standard taste of music, Madonna might not be as trend-setting as she was before (in the sense of influencing the general public) but she has definitely inspired and influenced a lot of new younger artists (through her music and image) to just be themselves regardless of any prejudices thrown at them. If only ageism was a thing of the past, I'm sure Madonna would have more success and influence than she has so far right now. And when ageism has finally become a thing of the past, we will have Madonna to thank for it.

content: I listen to this and suddenly it’s 1999 and I’m 16 again. That was a wonderful summer. I had to bike everywhere, to summer school and my yard jobs, cause my parents wouldn’t let me get my license. Early mornings in class, followed by yard work, then biking back to the local water park to cool off with my brothers and friends. No cell phone, social media bs, or any of that. Life was simple. Being sixteen and biking well over twenty miles a day and thinking nothing of it was wonderful. Good memories. Nowadays, I’d be terrified at the thought of letting my son do that when he’s sixteen. The world is a very different place now. I’d give almost anything to be able to go back for a week, or even a day. I didn’t know how good I had it. But I love the way that music can take us back to a certain point in time and bring those memories to the forefront of our minds, and make us smile and remember. When you’re sixteen, you think you’ll live forever and things seem to take too long to change. It isn’t until you’re older that time starts to fly by. That feeling of invincibility and feeling like I’d be young forever is a cherished memory now, but it’s an integral and essential part of growing up. Cheers, everyone ❤️

content: I remember this song like it was yesterday. As a young gay man 19yrs-old growing up in the 90"s. I was homeless, My mother kick me out. I was raised in an abusive household. the day she kicked me out was the first time I stood up for myself. I stayed with friends for 2 weeks until I met by ex-boyfriend at the time, He was 36 yrs-old at the time He took me in right away. we were together for almost three years. He became abusive and jealous. I felt trap and alienated from the world. I had nowhere to go. I had to endure the abuse. util one day I found out he was cheating on me. I was devastated and broken. I was lost, confused and alone. I had no one to talk to. The pain was unbearable. I wanted to end my life. One rainy night I was in the car (sobbing). This song came on the radio. It gave me the strength, courage, self-loved and worth. I moved out of Springfield, Ma to Orlando, Fl. best decision ever! 26 years later I am still here living my best life. Thank you- Madonna!

content:
To me, there is always a difference between an actual performance, or basically making a huge (and in this case succesful) play out of something you do.  I mean, they are both performances, but if the first has to do without the dance choreography and the second would have much, much more of an impact and would equally be as stunning to watch (if only for the wardrobe choices), I'd probably choose the later.
There is a place and a time for both of them, and a live performance in singing has it's own challenges that are not always easy or even possible to overcome at certain locations where the performance is held.
I think the main difference for many people is that this live performance still holds up really well in not only the choreography part, but also the way the video registration was made, while I sometimes feel some more modern performance acts really need superflashy light setups and an epileptically challenging video director pushing the buttons like crazy (or maybe even have this preprogrammed after numerous rehearsels) in order to actually "be" something.
In modern acts that have their technology fail them at one point you can quickly see how little is left when that happens, I think that that's what bothers some people when something is lip-synced, it didn't really require lip-syncing in the first place but because the act needs to be 1000% perfect there is no margin for even the slightest error.

She had taken a very specifically queer, transgender, Latino and African-American phenomenon and totally erased that context with her lyrics, 'it makes no difference if you're black or white, if you're a boy or a girl.' Madonna was taking in tons of money, while the Queen who actually taught her how to vogue sat before me in the club, strung out, depressed and broke. Love Lenny & Madonna, but as my name says: Let's have some Real Talk as indicated by other posts in different threads about this song.......Ingrid Chavez wrote this song, as her style from Prince's 1988 'Lovesexy' album inspired it. The song passed from her to Lenny, who changed the lyrics, who in turn 'gave it' to Madonna. But since neither officially credited her on the song credits, she sued in court.....and won. Prince would go on to put out Chavez' album because HER spoken-word style was unique at the time of the late 80s into the early 90s. Give the woman the credit she deserves and note the foresight Lenny had to 'borrow' the song while Madonna had the foresight to 'mainstream popularize' it into a memorable video concept.

EVOCATEUR EVOCATEUR I agreed w/everything that u said except the part about MJ being a bit past his prime. There is no such thing for MJ. He's not even in any league bc he's in a complete different league of his own. MJ is too iconic to even be judged based off of regular rules. Bc even around this time in 1990 as a matter of fact a year after this in 1991 when he released 'Black or white' off of his new 'Dangerous' album (which the 'Dangerous' tour sold out in a matter of minutes all around the world), the very beginning of the video similarly did the same thing w/the 'Black or white' video. The into where MJ is on top of a car dancing was premiered on MTV and also on freaking channel 2 or 4 I forget but it was a mainstream channel to premiere a music video?? Do u know what that was in 1991? I remember me and everyone in my house sat in front of the TV and waited for it to premiere. That was the first and LAST time that intro was shown. They cut it out of the video altogether bc ppl all over were calling in, writing in complaining about the  vulgarity in the intro bc MJ kept grabbing his crotch during the dance. Idk wth they expected from him bc he ALWAYS did that when he danced. But the controversy behind that was crazy. My aunt had taped it when it premiered so we got to watch it on tape but they refused to play it anywhere else. So MJ wasn't anywhere near past his prime when this song came out in 1990. Anyway, I also remember when Madonna released this video and song and it was a big uproar. I was only 7 years old at the time but I do remember overhearing the adults talking about it as well as ppl in TV talking about it. So yeah, it was a pretty huge deal back then.

Anytime I listen to "Vogue" by Madonna it makes me wish I could take a week off of work and jet off to Paris for fashion week in the fall. Of course I also think of the scene from "The Devil Wears Prada" where Anne Hathaway's character Andi Sachs is walking to work in different coats. A personal favorite coat of mine in that particular scene from the film is the green coat with leopard print fabric trim on the sleeve cuffs and collar. When I saw it for the first time I thought to myself "That coat looks like something Jackie Kennedy would have worn." Of course it looked just as great on Anne Hathaway as I am certain it would have looked on Jackie Kennedy.

content: I think this was the first song from her that I heard too

I was born in 1991 so I obviously don't have real life experience of the times, but this definitely seemed like it would be racy for the time. Especially the part where she lifts her skirt for one of the dancers! I honestly think her being relatively covered enhances the sexuality of the choreography. 

Also, to be clear, I think that when Karla talked about younger people not realizing how iconic Madonna was at the time, I don't think she meant that young people can't recognize her as an icon and an incredible artist. I think she means that it's hard to understand how radical her work seemed at the time if you weren't coming up in that climate. It's possible to gain that perspective, of course, but it's not unreasonable to think that most people who weren't around then don't have an automatic understanding of the cultural significance without having an interest and putting in the effort to learn about it.

Yes, I remember it like it was yesterday when my mom pulled all sorts of magical moves in order to bring me an audio cassette of "True Blue" in time for my 8th birthday on Sep 14, 1986, in a small town of Vukovar, Croatia. I just started the 2nd grade and I was the coolest kid in the class because at my birthday party besides the kick-ass banana-chocolate ice cream cake, sandwiches, Fanta & Coca-Cola in a can (that was a big deal in the 1980's Croatia/Yugoslavia because you couldn't just buy the cans in any store), I also had the hottest album at the time - Madonna's "True Blue". We must have played the tape about 150 times over and over, barely leaving anytime for some Wham or Spandau Ballet. Fast forward 35 years later I'm a 43 year old successful guy living in a luxury high rise on Chicago's Magnificent Mile, still listening to "True Blue" and still loving and even more appreciating my mom who did everything in her power to raise her only child as a single parent and she made sure I not only have all the necessary things such as food, clothes and ctr. but she went wayyyy beyond that. For me, True Blue album serves as the ultimate symbol of my mother's love and affection who made sure I had everything I needed and everything I ever wanted.  Not that she ever asked me for anything back, but today I make sure she has everything she needs and everything she wants. But the only thing she ever needs or wants is for me to be healthy and happy. I am very lucky to have a mom like that and I plan to spend as much time as possible with her for the remainder of our lives. I hope that many of you have had the same experience and if you haven't done it in a while please put on True Blue album in the background and call your mom:)

Before it was a thing? Yes...but so was Marilyn Monroe in the 40's with her gay makeup artist. His life was threatened on a daily basis and she fought to have him sit with her when eating dinner at clubs and restaurants.
Bette Midler and Barbra Streisand during the 60's and 70's were pro LGBT and had their lives and careers threatened over and over. Bette had glass and rocks thrown at her on stage and was nearly hit by a car in the street on her way home from someone who wanted to kill her. Liza only hired gay dancers for her shows in support during her early days and was constantly being harassed in the street with both men and women trying punch her.
Madonna did A LOT for the LBGT people but mainly the 90's generation talk about her support.
A whole two to three generations of LBGT people before the 90's had singers before her support them but no one today mentions them or gives them their much deserved credit. 
I hate young kids today thanking GAGA for breaking boundaries for the LGBT...young people need to learn their history of those who's lives were put on hold and put in danger for us. Gaga and Madonna are only on a long list of entertainers who helped open the minds of straight people and straight culture to open themselves and their beliefs about us. 
Not to mention CHER who was supporting us in the media during the 60's and 70's and nearly had the Sonny and Cher show cancelled after nightly complaints from viewers for supporting "The evil homosexuals" and "The sodimites (spelling) who were sending both her and Sonny to hell".
I just like to point out that before GAGA we had Madonna and she did so much for our community and really helped make a difference in pop culture to show us in a greater light. Fun Fact: Madonna was actually about 5 months pregnant with her son during the making of this video.
Hence the reason she is wearing that huge fur coat, and why there is no full frontal views of her.
If you look really hard though you can catch a glimpse of her baby bump.
When we did the 'Music' video, it was a weird time. [Madonna] was pregnant and we didn't want her to look pregnant—so we had to work around that. We had an idea to do a fun party video with her and her girls, make it a little bling and cowboy hats and all that. We wanted to have some comedy in there and I wanted to do some animation.
The video was shot in Los Angeles, California in April 2000, to accommodate Madonna's pregnancy and her then-growing waistline. Crewmember were asked to sign confidentiality documents for the filming. Madonna conceptualized the clip, wanting to show her take on the stereotypical R&B and rap music videos. She wanted to do role reversals with females enacting characters generally reserved for males. However, her range was limited being pregnant so she "had to think of a concept that would incorporate me being almost a voyeur rather than the central force in the video. So I figured if I played this kind of mack-daddy/pimp character, where things just came to me, happened to me and happened around me while I was watching it all happen, I could kill two birds with one stone."
Madonna's look in the clip was described by O'Brien as a "ghetto-fabulous female in a feather boa and stetson; all diamonds and bling, going to lapdancing bars and travelling in the back of a luxury limousine". The singer had initially enlisted some actors to portray her accompaniments in the video, but she did not feel comfortable with their stiffness, their model-like appearance and lack of personality. So she called up Mazar and Harris to come and star in the video. "I was at home when Madonna called me. She said 'I need my girlfriend, not these fake girl-friends trying to be my girlfriends. Throw on some clothes and come down'", recalled Harris. Ali G kept entertaining everyone in the set with Madonna not being able to "keep a straight face" during her takes. The singer kept her coat shut all the time and had to sit in the limousine to hide her belly.

This was not at ALL the tone at the time, I remember well. I had just graduated high school, and had been a Madonna fan since '84. None of the singles topped the Hot 100, a first for any Madonna album since her debut. The lead single, Frozen, was all over the radio, but Ray of Light, the second single, got half the airplay. The latter two US singles were next to never on the radio. The album was critically acclaimed, winning several grammy's, and it appeared on many best-of lists at the end of the year, but outside Madonna's fandom, the public criticized it for the very same things she's ALWAYS been criticized for, jumping late on trends. The ravers of the time, who'd been jamming to electronic music for a decade, lambasted her for making it mainstream and killing the scene, accusing her of trying to pretend to be Bjork. MTV did a news segment where they interviewed young people on spring break, many calling Madonna a phony, saying the record was a lame attempt at Madonna trying to get rich off whatever she thought was "trendy". She was accused of this with Bedtime Stories before, and Hard Candy afterwards. She was accused of making a paycheck off gay culture with Vogue. 
My point is that while I think Ray of Light is a Top 5 album from the 90s, and easily one of Madonna's top 3 albums in her catalog, its sound wasn't "revolutionary", it was merely revolutionary for HER. It was unexpected of HER. She was finishing her second decade in pop music, had just done a large scale musical and a ballad collection, and became a mom.. ppl expected her to become Celine Dion part duex. Instead she released a fully realized electronic album that was both sophisticated and spiritual. 
As others have said, Britney is wrongly considered an artist of the 90s, but she didn't start appearing on the radio until the last 6-8 months of the decade. She is essentially an Aught's artist, with all of her radio presence dominating the years 2000-2010. Radio in 1998, if you can recall, was pretty shitty. The renaissance in rock that began with Nirvana, known as the grunge or alternative scene, from the first half of the decade, was largely disappearing, and it was being filled with crap like Matchbox 20, Smashmouth, Semisonic, etc. Oh it was awful. 
The mid to late 90s had next to no dance music on the radio. If you wanted dance music, you went to the gay bars to hear the remixes of what was on the radio, or catch songs from Kristine W, Daft Punk, Junior Vasquez, Danny Tenaglia, Faithless... oh I could rant all day about those days. 
I digressed... sorry.

1987 At the sweet age of 5 I told my mother I wanted a bride costume for Halloween and gloves. I then cut it to shreds, the fingers out of the gloves, got a hold of my moms 80's super hold mousse whatever and showed up at my Catholic school for Halloween day very loudly and proudly proclaiming I was not a bride, I was Madonna in Like a Virgin.
And so the obsession began...
She was my idol, she was my everything. I read books, bought every album (sneaked Erotica out of a store, my mom found it and confiscated) taped the Bedtime Stories pajama party, eventually got to watch Truth or Dare. Saw Desperately Seeking Susan and Who's That Girl about 200 times.
I couldn't get enough. But after life caught up with me I fell out of touch after Ray of Light until Confessions on the Dancefloor. But then it faded again. I did bring her back to life with my daughter though with True Blue. I'm good with it.

Mia Chevalier RockAmerica is a music video subscription service for professional disc jockeys based in New York City, New York. Founded in 1980 by Ed Steinberg, it was the first company to offer music videos on a subscription basis, and provided a vital channel before the establishment of MTV and other television outlets.

Founded on the heels of successful record pools that would distribute disco and new wave records to DJs, the company applied the same principle to music video but with important differences. The videos, which were obtained from record labels both major and independent, were gathered into compilations. Clubs could subscribe and receive fresh tapes on a monthly basis. However the tapes had to be returned at the end of two months. Further conditions were dictated by copyright concerns. Clubs were not supposed to play the reels continuously but to mix them into other programming. The clubs were also mandated to provide monthly 'response forms' to RockAmerica from which the company produced a video chart, as well as more detailed analysis that could be purchased by marketers. The first program included videos by artists Madness, XTC, David Bowie, The Flying Lizards, The Ramones and Ian Dury. The company rapidly signed up a number of east coast clubs.[1] The company also produced videos for a number of NYC artists including the Bush Tetras and, notably, the video for Madonna's debut single Everybody. By the summer of 1983 RockAmerica had 300 subscribers.[2]

In its early days Bob Pittman of MTV based his programming on RockAmerica's chart.[3]

The company remained influential in the 1990s playing a crucial role in breaking the boy band phenomenon.[4]

In 2005 RockAmerica was acquired by the Retail Entertainment Design company. In 2007 Steinberg was made a Vice President of RockAmerica’s owner.[5]

Song name 
Gimme Gimme Gimme

It's both Marlene Dietrich in "The Blue Angel" (she was bisexual, but didn't use that word) and a sly insinuation of Rita Hayworth, with the move using the glove ("Gilda"). Her outfit (Lacroix?) resembles Rita's famous dress during the drunken dance scene in "Gilda". This video is timeless, to those who notice such things.

More on the "Gilda" theme: Rita Hayworth's character was seen as a "whore" by her ex. When the movie opens, he's been recruited to run a gambling operation for what turns out to be Gilda's husband. The ex hates Gilda and still loves her, at the same time. Their angsty relationship in the movie, inspired this song, "Open Your Heart", it seems to me.

Also, Rita and Glenn Ford might have had a similar relationship, offscreen.

Yes, in 2025.

Madonna is Madonna. She is certainly not a scam. She has written, co-written, co-produced and recorded some of the greatest pop music of the last 30+ years. Her videos are the stuff of legend. Her live performances are awesome from the very first Virgin Tour through Blond Ambition, Girlie Show, Confessions up to the last MDNA tour. She is an underrated pop melodist and lyricist. People like Nile Rodgers, Rick Nowels, David Forster, Mirwais etc are all on record saying just this. But people hate to think some little white lady has that much creativity in her. I'm an ex-dancer and dance teacher and I bow down to Madonna as a technically brilliant dancer with that extra special something. Her vocals in the studio, particularly on her ballads, are gorgeous. Her voice is utterly unique and distinctive. The True Blue and Like a Prayer albums are classics in the pop pantheon and Ray of Light and Confessions on a Dance Floor rank among the greatest and most cohesive pop albums of the last 15 years. Obviously one can dislike Madonna and her music but she is the real deal and she achieved all this long before Youtube and the internet. I saw her in December 1982 and January 1983 at Danceteria and she was the best thing there those nights. The only good thing there, actually!

Is it a fact that "stupid" people are actually more dangerous than criminals? Are families as strong as they were say 40 years ago or are they falling apart in comparison to the past. How about our schools and education programs..... are the children ok? Perhaps these stupid people have sneaky..... street "survival instincts" , or a "code of the street mentality" always looking for the next so called "hustle" even if they are falling deeper and deeper into an unhealthy sugar comma because they hate fruits and vegetables but love extra grease, candy and heavily processed and preserved foods. I know this is philosophical ; but, thinking about this is good for people. People need to be observant and experience their surroundings and compare, analyze and contrast things in their world. I saw something on YouTube about how young people in the 1960's were very fit in comparison to young people today and how does a "stark lack of fitness" affect thinking, mental and physical functioning and action. You see, this world will need more and more "doers" and less and less "complainers" or else the future will be very bleak indeed. Madonna was already huge at the time and what a lot of people--fans, music critics, etc.--didn't realize is what was yet to come.  Madonna was already in mid-20's and certainly not young, like say Britney Spears or Billie Eilish, when she became hot.  So in essence, she wasn't a teen idol.  The fangirls (and me) saw something else entirely.  They say not themselves represented in the now, but what they aspired to be in the future.

content:

Like I said, the trend that Madonna has started as of right now is fighting against ageism in all its forms and shapes. As a person who doesn’t follow a certain rule but makes her own rule, Madonna looks, dresses and sounds exactly as she pleases. Being the influential person that she is, she is now setting a new trend on fighting the ageist battles for the benefit of all female artists. At the same time, she is paving the way so that younger artists won't have endure the beating in the future. Madonna’s refusal to be affected by age is inspiring, so much so that other artists and celebrities (young and old) are also following her footsteps. Lately, more and more people are speaking their mind and voicing their opinion on the issue of ageism. Through their action and continuing support, this has now become a trend. Another ongoing trend that Madonna started way back when it wasn’t cool to do so, was standing up for the LGBT community and fighting for human rights and peace. Even today many new younger artists and celebrities are still following Madonna's footsteps. They are still fighting for human rights and standing up for the LGBT community throughout the world, so much so that it has become a trend to promote the message of tolerance and love in their songs, videos and concerts. Talking about concerts, if you see Miley Cyrus or Nicki Minaj or Lady Gaga or Katy Perry dance provocatively on stage, don’t forget the ground breaking performance that started the trend: Madonna’s super sexy rendition of “Like A Virgin” at the 1984 VMAs. Whenever you see Taylor Swift’s or Ariana Grande’s, or Beyonce’s, or Rihanna’s midriff during their performance, wearing crop tops, cut-off t-shirts, and sometimes just bra, don’t forget that the trend was started by Madonna. And whenever you see today’s artists wearing a microphone headset while performing on stage, don’t forget that the trend was popularized by Madonna. Madonna is all about making statement and you can see this through her style, outfits and accessories, all of which are still trendy. If you want to know more trends that Madonna made popular when she was younger, you have to do the reading yourself.

It wasn't that. They felt she was overly saturated with hits already at the time of the song, so it got relegated to the "B" side of the 33' singles released for 'Angel' (LAV album). Back then, we had record players instead of digital singles like today. A record has two sides that stores songs, so, on a 33" record, you would have two songs (one on each side).  It was a huge mistake in my opinion, for this song was huge in my region of Canada.

A humble suggestion to Madonna:
Album name: ‘Zephyr in the Sky’
An appeal by the fans to release a compilation of 20 masterpieces from Madonna’s catalogue personifying every emotion we have been through in this most dark age. And as we steer back to humanity and life. Search for the future.
A journey to the light, there is always a ray of hope and then the brilliant radiance of life.
Emotions and these songs clutch in perpetuity, the journey we continue.
 
 
Ray of Light
The deliverance of hope. The rhythm of life never seizes. There will always be that zephyr in the sky each night. Embrace it we can.
 
Masterpiece
Preservation of our most precious in peril. Our children, our people. Our kind. Caress them, protect them, enliven them.
 
Crazy
Souls without direction. Souls succumbing to the anarchy. Moments of lapse and dissolution of reason. There is always a method in any madness.
 
Miles Away
Separation - a reality, distancing in the new remote ecosystem – a way of life. Now from a far. Life anew.
 
4 Minutes
There were heroes. And there were those who saved the world, those who ultimately will and those who arrogantly don’t. We can all be heroes, for even more than a day.
 
Borderline
Barriers, walls, borders can neither prevent the triumphant human, nor conquer the invisible enemy. Humanity on the borderline of distraught. Never destroyed.
 
Die Another Day
Death can never be normalized. Not one, not a million. Fight. Death will come, in victory there will ultimately be death. But another day, its not our time to go.
 
You’ll See
Strength is personal, strength is contagion, strength prevails, strength is now our constant. You’ll see.
 
La Isla Bonita
My dreams are always my own and even I don’t control them. There will be sun again, there will be celebration again, we will hold hands again –into that beautiful sunset.
 
Ghost Town
The cradles of our civilization – devoid of voices, people, laughter, love. Ghostly in silence, our great cities shall live again – thrive they shall.
 
Like a Prayer
God. Life is a mystery, everyone must stand alone. You will hear the voice and I always hear you call my name. Forgive me. Pray.
 
Joan of Arc
You’re only human. Our weakness it can never be. A Joan in us all. New awakenings.
 
Sorry
Not so many need to be sorry. But sorry are those who remain bigoted in normalizing death and cheating life. They must be sorry. Usher in the preservers. Change.
 
Another Suitcase in Another Hall
In the anarchy of this world, don’t lose yourself. ‘I’ never goes out of fashion. You are not another brick in the wall.
 
Dress You Up
Making love, satiated the nights of darkness, all over, all over my body, mind and soul. The adrenaline of sex, of lust, of expressing it still.
 
Vogue
At night we lock the door when no one else can see. There was a time with my people around me. I’m good with me for now. I n deed to dance, you can too.
 
Human Nature
Express yourself don’t repress yourself. Humans we are humane but he not always is. The Emperors of yore and now shall not be remembered – not those who lacked humanity and mocked death.
 
I rise
I am talking about a revolution. A revolution of love. Of compassion. Of kindness. Our time is now and we shall not let the autumn leaves fall in vain. All rise and realize your own renaissance – for you and for the world.
 
I’ll Remember
I’ll remember the love. I’ll remember the love you gave me. Now that we’re standing on our own – we shall walk again together, we shall celebrate again together. Another circus, another show.
Music
Its about the music. Its always been about the music. Music helped me breathe this, live this, survive this. Music makes the people come together – yeah.
 
In gratitude, Madonna.

content: @Invisible Emo If you are talking about this song, it came out two years before the new millenium began so, yes, it was still the Nineties.  As far as the decade itself is concerned, optimism and peace were not at an "all time high".  The Cold War ended before the 90's really began, so you cannot use that as an example for optimism and peace.  When the 90's did roll in, there was the Rodney King incident, followed by massive riots.  There was also the controversial law enforcement siege on the Branch Davidian compound in Waco, Texas.  Too, the largest domestic terrorist attack in the U.S. occurred in the 90's - the Oklahoma City bombing.  As well, the World Trade Center was bombed by international terrorists during the 90's, 19 service members were killed in Somalia, and the First Gulf War occurred when the decade began.  The only optimism associated with the 1990's came from the economy; the astonishing onset of the Internet and the outburst of technical industries that came with it, made the decade a prosperous one.  On the heels of that, the only music backdrop that could possibly be correlated with the Nineties would be the Grunge movement.  In short, Madonna's song (Ray of Light) does not "sum up the 90's", and it's utterly ridiculous to think that it even could.  'Ray of Light' is a magnificent tune which just happened to come out during the latter part of that decade.

I was 10. I'm 34 now. This album had such an incredibly profound impact on me as a kid and it's astonishing how much this album still plays an integral part of who I am. The opening riff and synths of this song....man! I discovered myself to this record. 5th grade was brutal. Especially when you start realizing you're gay. Ray of Light was my solace. I finally saw her perform it a decade later in Philadelphia for The Sticky and Sweet Tour. My heart was ready to burst out of my chest I jumping so hard. My throat was burning from screaming so loud. This song catapulted me into super fandom. It introduced me to electronica and club music. That would lead me to artist like Daft Punk. Groove Armada, Groovejet, Armand Van Helden, Modjo etc. Electronica then led me to disco and ultimately to my greatest love of all, HOUSE! I scoured the internet, still in its' infancy, for the teeniest tiniest samples of remixes to all the singles. I was obviously way too young for the clubs, so I'd stay up late on Saturday nights and listen to Q102, Philadelphia's number 1 hit music station, where they'd have DJ Richie Rich broadcast his live set from a club called Egypt on the Waterfront. I would record the remixes on cassette tape and play them out! I was poor and the oldest of four. My single mom with three jobs wasn't trying to buy me a new cassette every week so I had to carefully choose which songs I would record over and try hard not to record over the Madonna remixes. God, what a time!
And she's still filling arenas and making new fans. Amazing woman!
